w:r.w:t: Senior Engineering Review — No-App, Browser-First Architecture
Updated: Payments and WhatsApp deferred to future phase
2. Why the QR Approach is the Right Call
3. Critical Gaps in the Current Design
4. MVP v1 — What to Actually Build

This document refines the original Xerox Shop design by replacing the customer mobile app with a no-install, QR-based web flow. A customer at a print shop scans a QR code, opens a shop-specific web page, uploads a PDF, picks options, and submits the order. The shop owner sees the order in a web admin panel with audio alerts and prints directly. The customer pays the shop in person at pickup, exactly as they do today.

The original two-app design is correct in spirit but heavy in execution. Customer-side app installs are a major drop-off in the Indian print-shop market, where most users print only a few times a year. Removing the install step is the single highest-leverage change available.

This plan keeps the strong parts of the original document (admin panel, order workflow, cloud storage, status tracking) and rebuilds the customer side as a fast, anonymous, QR-driven web flow. It closes the critical gaps in the original around page-count-based pricing, shop notification reliability, and abuse protection.
Two features that were considered in the previous version of this plan have been deliberately deferred so v1 stays small and shippable:
v1 will use cash or in-person UPI at the shop counter, exactly as shops operate today. Online prepayment will be added in v2.

v1 will use a customer-facing order status URL instead. The customer bookmarks or revisits the URL to see status. The shop can call the customer directly when ready. WhatsApp Business API integration will be added in v2.
Built to the v1 scope below, this product can launch with one cooperative shop in 2–3 weeks and scale to several shops without architectural changes.
2. Why the QR Approach is the Right Call
Three reasons make a no-app, QR-driven flow the right primary funnel for this product:

Print shops serve users who need to print 2–10 times a year. Asking them to install an app, sign up, verify OTP, grant location permission, and learn a new UI is too much friction for the value delivered. App store reviews of every comparable product show this pattern.
2.2 QR codes are universally understood in India
UPI normalised QR scanning across all demographics and age groups. A user in any tier-2 or tier-3 city can scan a QR without instructions. The mental model already exists.

One web codebase replaces three (Android app, iOS app, web). No Play Store reviews, no App Store reviews, no version fragmentation. A bug fix ships in minutes, not days. This matters enormously in the first 6 months while the product is finding its shape.
No customer login means no order history or saved preferences (acceptable for v1).
Users cannot place orders remotely from home (deferred to v2 via shop-discovery page).
No automatic ready notification to customer (replaced by status URL plus manual phone call from shop).
3. Critical Gaps in the Current Design
The original design omits three issues that will break the product in real-world use. These must be addressed in v1.
A browser-based admin panel fails silently when the tab is closed, the laptop sleeps, or the network drops. If the shop misses three orders, customers lose trust and the platform dies.

Loud audio alarm and visual flash on the admin tab, plus a browser-tab title that updates with the pending order count. Shop owner is trained to keep the admin tab open during business hours, exactly like a POS terminal. Optional SMS alert to the shop owner's phone can be added in v1.1 if needed.
Showing a final price requires knowing the page count. PDFs allow this server-side in milliseconds. DOCX, PPTX, and image conversions are slow, fragile, and expensive.
Restrict v1 uploads to PDF only. Use pdf-lib or pdf-parse on the backend to extract the page count, then compute price = pages × copies × per-page rate (with a colour multiplier).

A no-login system can be spammed with fake orders. Without protection, a single bad actor can flood a shop's queue or run up cloud storage costs. Without online payment in v1, this risk is amplified — a bad actor faces zero financial cost to spam.

Phone OTP verification before order submission (one-time, not a login). Rate-limit by IP (5 orders/hour) and by phone (3 active orders maximum). All files virus-scanned before storage. Shop owner is the human gate — they accept or reject each order before any printing happens, so wasted paper is minimised.
4. MVP v1 — What to Actually Build
The goal of v1 is the smallest set of features that one shop can use end-to-end. Everything not on this list is deferred.
Customer scans QR at shop. Opens https://printshop.in/s/{shopId}.
Page loads with shop name, photo, and a single 'Upload PDF' button.
Customer selects a PDF file (max 25 MB). Backend extracts page count.
Customer chooses options: B&W or Colour, copies (1–50), single or double-sided, optional notes.
Live price displays as options change.
Customer enters name and phone number. Phone OTP is sent and verified.
Customer clicks Submit. Order is created with status PLACED.
Confirmation page shows order ID, estimated time, and a status URL the customer can revisit.
Customer collects prints at the shop and pays in cash or in-person UPI.
Email + password login with remember-me option.
Single-screen dashboard: pending orders count, today's order count, printer status.
New orders appear instantly with audio alarm + visual flash + browser tab title change.
Each order card shows: customer name, masked phone, page count, copies, colour, total amount, instructions.
Inline PDF preview — no download needed.
Status buttons: Accept → Printing → Ready → Completed.
Mark Completed when customer collects and pays.
Daily summary at end of day (orders completed, pages printed, expected cash collected).
Public URL like /order/{shortToken} accessible without login.
Shows current status, shop name and address, total amount to pay at pickup.
Refreshes automatically every 30 seconds, or instantly via real-time updates.
Shop owner has the customer's phone number and can call when prints are ready.
View all shops and all orders.
Online payments (UPI prepayment, Razorpay) — deferred to v2.
DOCX, PPTX, image uploads (PDF only in v1).
Binding, paper-size choices, A3 / Letter / Legal.
In-app chat between customer and shop.
Multi-language UI (English only in v1; Hindi/Telugu in v1.1).
Every order moves through a fixed sequence of states. Each state has explicit entry and exit conditions, and each transition triggers a side effect (status update, file deletion, etc.).
File uploaded, options selected, OTP not yet verified.
Customer verifies OTP and clicks Submit.
Order submitted and waiting in shop's queue. Audio alert fires in admin panel.
Shop has acknowledged the order. Status URL updates.
Prints ready for pickup. Status URL shows Ready. Shop calls customer if needed.
Customer arrives, pays at counter, shop clicks Completed.
Order picked up and paid. Files scheduled for deletion in 24h.
Order rejected by shop or marked no-show after 48h.
DRAFT not submitted within 30 minutes → auto-deleted.
PLACED not accepted within 2 hours → escalation alert in admin panel; super admin notified.
READY not picked up within 48 hours → auto-cancelled; files scheduled for deletion.
COMPLETED + 24 hours → file permanently deleted from cloud storage.
The original document proposed React + Node + MongoDB. That works, but a few substitutions reduce complexity and cost for a small team. Final recommendation below.
Single repo, API routes, SSR for fast QR-scan loads on slow networks. One deployment.
Fast iteration, mobile-first by default, small bundle.
Data is relational (shops, orders). Foreign keys, transactions, and reporting are simpler than in MongoDB.
Signed URLs, lifecycle rules for auto-delete, low latency for India.
Hosted WebSocket service for new-order alerts and status URL updates. Avoids running Socket.io infrastructure in v1.
India-focused, ~₹0.15 per SMS, DLT compliant. Used only for phone verification in v1.
Server-side page count extraction. Fast and reliable for standard PDFs.
Zero infra ops in v1. Move to AWS only when traffic justifies it.
Generate as PNG/SVG on demand. Embed in shop welcome kit.
Catches issues in production before customers complain.

Four tables cover the full v1 product. Add columns later as features grow; do not over-design the schema upfront. Payment-related tables and columns are intentionally left out and will be added in v2.
id, name, slug (used in QR URL), owner_email, password_hash, phone, address, lat, lng, price_bw_per_page, price_color_per_page, is_approved, is_open, created_at, updated_at.

id, short_token (used in customer status URL), shop_id (FK), customer_name, customer_phone, customer_phone_verified, file_s3_key, page_count, copies, color (bool), double_sided (bool), notes, total_amount, order_status (enum from §5.1), created_at, updated_at, status_history (jsonb array of {status, at}).
id, phone, code_hash, expires_at, attempts, verified, created_at. Auto-purged after 24 hours.
id, actor_type (customer/shop/admin/system), actor_id, action, target_type, target_id, payload (jsonb), created_at. Critical for debugging order issues and disputes.
Whitelist PDFs only by content sniff (not just extension).
Maximum size: 25 MB. Enforced at upload-presign time.
Virus scan with ClamAV before the file is committed to the orders table.
S3 signed URLs with 30-minute expiry; never expose raw bucket paths.
S3 lifecycle rule: delete files 24 hours after order COMPLETED status.
Shop owner: bcrypt-hashed password, JWT access tokens, refresh tokens with rotation.
Customer: phone OTP with 6-digit code, 5-minute expiry, max 5 attempts.
Rate limit per IP (60 req/min general, 5 orders/hour) using Vercel Edge or Upstash.
Customer status URL uses an unguessable short_token, not the order ID.
DPDP Act 2023 compliance: clear consent on the upload page, retention policy stated.
Customer phone number masked in shop-side view (e.g., 98••••3210); shop owner can reveal it to call when prints are ready.
Files are visible only to the assigned shop and the customer who uploaded them.
Quarterly review of database access; least-privilege roles for service accounts.
Public shop page, PDF upload, page-count, live price calculator. No DB, no OTP. The artefact you show prospective shops.
Full scope from §4. One shop in production. Phone OTP via MSG91. Admin panel with audio alerts. Customer pays in person at pickup.
Bug fixes from real shop feedback. Hindi/Telugu UI. Onboard 3–5 more shops. Super-admin shop approval flow polished. Optional SMS alerts to shop owner phone for new orders.
Razorpay UPI prepayment integration. WhatsApp Business API for ready notifications and shop-owner alerts. Customer order history (optional login).
Shop discovery page (search by location). DOCX/image upload via server-side PDF conversion. Reorder. Ratings and reviews.
Home delivery integration. Bulk corporate accounts. Subscription plans. Analytics for shops. WhatsApp-only ordering bot.
Shop misses a new-order alert (browser tab closed)
Audio alarm, visual flash, and tab title with pending count. Shop trained to keep tab open during business hours. Optional SMS alert added in v1.1.
Shop reviews and accepts each order before printing — wasted paper minimised. Auto-cancel READY orders after 48h. v2 prepayment will eliminate the financial risk entirely.
Files visible only to assigned shop. Auto-delete 24h after pickup. Signed URLs expire in 30 min. Quarterly access audit.
Phone OTP, IP rate limiting, 3-active-order cap per phone, ClamAV virus scan, shop human review before printing.
Customer doesn't know order is ready
Status URL shown on confirmation. Shop has customer phone and calls when ready. WhatsApp notifications added in v2.
Multipart upload with retry. Progress bar. 25 MB cap. CDN-backed presigned URL.
S3 lifecycle auto-deletes files 24h after pickup. Hard cap on uploads per phone per day.
Track these from day one. They tell you whether the product is working.
Median time from order placed to order accepted (target: under 2 minutes).
Median time from accepted to ready (target: under 10 minutes for small orders).
Customer pickup rate (placed → completed). Target: above 85%.
Rejected order rate (placed → cancelled by shop). Target: below 5%.
Active shops (placed at least one order in last 7 days).
Orders per shop per day (target: 5+ within first month).
Total pages printed per day across all shops.
Cash collected per shop (reported by shop owner; reconciled in v2 with online payment).
Recommended order of execution. Each step builds on the previous one and produces something visible.
Set up Next.js project, Tailwind, PostgreSQL on Neon, S3 bucket in Mumbai region.
Build the public shop page (/s/[slug]) with PDF upload, page-count extraction, and live price calculator. This is the demo you show the first prospective shop owner.
Implement phone OTP via MSG91 and store verifications in the otp_verifications table.
Build order submission flow: from verified OTP to PLACED status, with the customer status URL.
Build the shop admin panel: login, dashboard, order list, status updates, inline PDF preview.
Add real-time new-order alerts via Pusher: audio alarm, visual flash, browser tab title change.
Build the customer status page (/order/{shortToken}) with live status updates.
Build the super-admin shop approval flow.
Add Sentry, Vercel Analytics, and a daily summary job.
Pilot with one cooperative shop. Monitor every order. Fix bugs as they appear.
After 100 successful orders at the pilot shop, onboard 3 more shops.
Once stable, begin v2: Razorpay prepayment + WhatsApp notifications.
The first cooperative shop is the hardest milestone. Once it works end-to-end with real customers, the rest of the product follows quickly.
